content: Vaccines prime adaptive immunity by presenting antigenic determinants in a controlled context that engages both innate sensing and antigen presentation pathways. This initiates clonal expansion of naive B and T lymphocytes specific to vaccine-encoded epitopes, facilitated by dendritic cell maturation and optimized co-stimulatory signaling. The resultant germinal center reactions refine B cell affinity via somatic hypermutation and class-switch recombination, fostering a high-affinity memory B cell pool and long-lived plasma cells. Concurrently, CD4+ T follicular helper cells support this humoral maturation, while CD8+ T cells develop cytotoxic memory subsets when intracellular antigen processing occurs. Importantly, vaccine-induced innate immune activation—through pattern recognition receptors or adjuvants—modulates epigenetic and metabolic reprogramming, enhancing the quality and durability of memory. Different platforms, such as mRNA vaccines, uniquely influence antigen expression kinetics and innate activation, shaping immune imprinting. Collectively, these processes establish a poised, antigen-experienced repertoire capable of rapid, robust secondary responses upon pathogen encounter.
